The Forgotten Ways

Alan Hirsch

  8. 8 The Apostolic Genius

Part Two

The DNA of Movemental Church

18 min read

Every significant movement in history has had at its core a set of ideas and practices that define its essential nature — what we might call its mDNA. This concept of movemental DNA helps us understand the intrinsic, reproducible, guiding mechanism of apostolic movement. Just as biological DNA carries the coding for the whole organism, so too there is a simple but comprehensive coding that seems to carry the potential of the whole movement within it.

What is remarkable about the phenomenon of exponential people movements is not merely their size — although that is certainly impressive — but rather that they seem to be driven by a common set of internal principles. When we strip away the cultural particulars, the historical contexts, the specific personalities involved, we begin to see a pattern so consistent that it demands explanation.

The early church, the Chinese underground movement, various missionary movements in history — all of these share what I call Apostolic Genius: the built-in life force and guiding mechanism of God's people. It is this that accounts for the extraordinary power and reach of these movements, and it is this that we desperately need to recover if we hope to see similar impact in our own time and place.

Six Elements of mDNA

When analyzed closely, Apostolic Genius expresses itself through six interdependent elements. These are not programs to implement or strategies to adopt; they are more like the essential characteristics that define the very nature of the church when it is operating in its most authentic, powerful mode.

The first element is Jesus Is Lord — the simple but all-encompassing confession that lies at the heart of every authentic Jesus movement. The implications of this confession, when taken seriously and worked out consistently, are nothing short of revolutionary. It affects how we understand authority, power, truth, community, ethics, and mission.

The second element is Disciple Making. Movements grow because they make disciples who make disciples. This is not merely about programs of instruction; it is about a form of spiritual apprenticeship that produces mature followers capable of reproducing the pattern in others. Without this, growth is addition at best; with it, growth becomes multiplication.

The Reproducibility Principle

What makes mDNA so powerful is its inherent reproducibility. Unlike institutional forms that require significant resources, trained professionals, and elaborate infrastructure to replicate, movemental DNA can reproduce in virtually any context. A small group of committed disciples, armed with the essential gospel message and empowered by the Spirit, can plant the seeds of movement anywhere.

This is why the most powerful movements in history have typically emerged not from the centers of institutional power but from the margins — from places of poverty, persecution, and apparent powerlessness. The very conditions that seem most hostile to institutional religion often prove most fertile for movemental Christianity.

Understanding this principle radically reframes how we think about church, mission, and leadership. It suggests that our primary task is not building bigger and better institutions but rather cultivating the conditions in which mDNA can flourish. It means investing in people rather than programs, in relationships rather than structures, in the essential over the elaborate.
